overview: Generates a list of all characters used in the translatable text of a given set of documents. The output is a tab-delimited file listing each character's Unicode code point, representation, and occurrence count. Useful for creating minimal bitmap fonts (e.g., Japanese game interfaces) by including only characters actually present in the text. Text units marked as non-translatable are ignored.

outputPath.help: Full path where the tab-delimited character listing will be written. The file contains three columns: Unicode code point (e.g., `U+0079`), character representation (e.g., `'y'`), and occurrence count.
outputPath.values: A file system path. The file will be created or overwritten. Default filename is `charlist.txt`.
examples: `charlist.txt` — relative path, written to the working directory

autoOpen.help: Automatically opens the result file in the system's default text editor/viewer after the step completes. Convenient for interactive use; disable for batch/automated pipelines.

processingNotes: Only translatable text units are scanned — text units marked as non-translatable are skipped.
The output list is **not in any specific order**.
The result file is **tab-delimited** with three columns: Unicode code point, character representation, and occurrence count.

examples.title: Character listing output format
examples.description: Example of the tab-delimited output file produced by the step. Each line shows the Unicode code point, the character in quotes, and how many times it appeared across all translatable text.

examples.title: Bitmap font optimization
examples.description: Use this step when building a bitmap font for a game interface (e.g., Japanese characters). By feeding your localized game strings through this step, you get the exact set of characters needed, allowing you to exclude unused glyphs and reduce font file size.
